name: Torrejón el Rubio from the Tejo River – circular through Monfragüe National Park
name_original: Torrejón el Rubio desde el río Tejo – circular por Parque Nacional de Monfragüe
region_intro: Touring cycling around Embalse de Alcántara is characterized by diverse landscapes, including the expansive Embalse de Alcántara reservoir and sections of the Tagus River. The terrain features rolling hills, rocky areas, and steppe environments, often traversing characteristic holm-oak and cork-oak forests. The Sierra de San Pedro mountains provide a scenic backdrop, and routes frequently pass through the Tajo International Natural Park, a biosphere reserve. Best touring cycling routes around Embalse de Alcántara The most popular touring cycling route is Torrejón el Rubio from the Tejo River – circular through Monfragüe National Park, a 25.0 miles (40.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 1 minute to complete. This difficult route offers a circular journey through the natural park, following the Tejo River. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is City tour Alcántara – circular by Embalse de José María de Oriol - Alcántara II – Parque Natural del Tagus Internacional, a difficult 18.5 miles (29.8 km) path. This route provides a circular tour around the town of Alcántara and the Embalse de José María de Oriol, within the Tajo International Natural Park. Local touring cyclists also love the Villarreal de San Carlos – Collado del Lobo loop from Aparcamiento de la fuente del francés, a 23.7 miles (38.1 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 41 minutes. Touring cycling around Embalse de Alcántara is defined by extensive reservoir views, holm-oak and cork-oak forests, and rolling hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Embalse de Alcántara are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 27 reviews. More than 284 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Embalse de Alcántara's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when touring cycling around Embalse de Alcántara?
A: The region offers diverse terrain, including rolling hills, rocky sections, and steppe areas, often traversing characteristic holm-oak and cork-oak forests. You'll also encounter extensive reservoir views and sections along the Tagus River, with the Sierra de San Pedro mountains providing a scenic backdrop.

Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in the Embalse de Alcántara area?
A: There are over 35 touring cycling routes available around Embalse de Alcántara. These routes cater to various skill levels, though the majority are rated as difficult, with a few moderate options.

Q: Are there any family-friendly cycling routes around Embalse de Alcántara?
A: While many routes in the area are rated as difficult, there are some moderate options that might be suitable for families with older children or those accustomed to longer rides. For example, consider routes that stick to flatter sections near the reservoir or within the Tajo International Natural Park, which offers a peaceful environment away from heavy traffic.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or natural features to see along the cycling routes?
A: Cyclists can explore a wealth of natural and historical attractions. You'll find impressive viewpoints such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3789378" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Alcántara Reservoir and Almonte Viaduct&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5577981" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoint of the Alcántara Reservoir and Bridges over the Almonte River&lt;/a&gt;. Natural monuments like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5679306" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Gypsy's Leap&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4446905" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Confluence of the Tagus and Tiétar rivers&lt;/a&gt; are also accessible. The historic Roman Bridge of Alcántara and the town's monumental heritage are also significant points of interest.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Embalse de Alcántara?
A: The Extremadura region, including Embalse de Alcántara, is ideal for touring cycling during spring and autumn. These seasons offer pleasant temperatures, making for comfortable rides. Summers can be very hot, while winters are generally mild but can have cooler, wetter days.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1105601593/torrejon-el-rubio-from-the-tejo-river-circular-through-monfraguee-national-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torrejón el Rubio from the Tejo River – circular through Monfragüe National Park&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a challenging journey through the natural park, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1114013055/city-tour-alcantara-circular-by-embalse-de-jose-maria-de-oriol-alcantara-ii-parque-natural-del-tagus-internacional" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;City tour Alcántara – circular by Embalse de José María de Oriol - Alcántara II – Parque Natural del Tagus Internacional&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Embalse de Alcántara?
A: The touring cycling routes around Embalse de Alcántara are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 27 reviews. Cyclists often praise the tranquil environment, diverse landscapes, and the opportunity to explore both natural beauty and historical sites away from mass tourism.

Q: Are there options for longer, more challenging touring cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The region is well-suited for longer, more demanding rides. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17314892" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Alambique Viewpoint – Serradilla loop from Barco de Monfragüe&lt;/a&gt; covers over 63 km with significant elevation gain, offering a substantial challenge. Another long option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40353971" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Gypsy's Leap – Villarreal de San Carlos loop from Barco de Monfragüe&lt;/a&gt;, spanning over 83 km.

Q: Is the region suitable for different types of touring bikes, like road, gravel, or mountain bikes?
A: Yes, the diverse terrain, which includes both paved and unpaved sections, makes the region suitable for various bike types. While road bikes can enjoy the quieter paved roads, gravel and mountain bikes will be better suited for exploring the extensive network of trails through holm-oak forests and more rugged sections.

Q: What are the typical elevation gains on touring cycling routes in this area?
A: Given the region's rolling hills and mountainous backdrops like the Sierra de San Pedro, many routes feature significant elevation gains. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14706251" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villarreal de San Carlos – Collado del Lobo loop from Aparcamiento de la fuente del francés&lt;/a&gt; includes over 900 meters of ascent, providing a good workout for touring cyclists.

Q: Can I find amenities like cafes or accommodation along the routes?
A: While the region offers a tranquil environment away from mass tourism, the town of Alcántara and other smaller villages along or near the routes provide opportunities for cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. Some organized tours in Extremadura also offer support like luggage transfer and a selection of guesthouses or paradors.

Q: Are there any specific regulations for cycling within the Tajo International Natural Park?
A: The Tajo International Natural Park is a protected biosphere reserve. While cycling is generally permitted on designated trails and roads, it's always advisable to respect local regulations, stay on marked paths, and minimize your impact on the environment. Specific permits are not typically required for general cycling, but always check for any updated local guidelines.
tips: Here you can watch the vultures very well. Definitely take a good pair of binoculars with you. It's fascinating when the vultures glide through the air using the thermals.

Torrejón el Rubio is an excellent destination for those seeking unspoiled nature, wildlife viewing, hiking, and peaceful tourism surrounded by the privileged environment of Monfragüe National Park, one of the best places in Spain for spotting birds such as the black vulture and the Spanish imperial eagle.

Furthermore, its historical heritage, the friendliness of the locals, and its strategic location make it a very complete place for a weekend getaway or as a base for exploring Monfragüe.

Nice country hotel. It was very quiet in March. Very good kitchen (evenings). Breakfast is standard, you have a nice view outside through panoramic windows. It is 9 km to the highlight of the Monfragüe National Park, the rock with the many griffon vultures (Salto Del Gitano; bring binoculars!), but also to the Castillo de Monfragüe Castle with a fantastic 360-degree panoramic view (and rock paintings accessible by entrance fee). It goes downhill in serpentines for almost 100 m. Near the Hospederia there is also a bridge of Roman origin within walking distance.

Monfragüe Castle was built by Muslim rulers during their occupation of the area, the castle dominates the landscape and watches out for anyone trying to pass through the narrow river gorge. From one of the remaining towers you will have incredible views of the Monfragüe National Park below.

Beautiful landscape at the confluence of the two rivers. The Tietar flows into the Tajo about 1 km south of the hamlet (pedanía) of Villarreal de San Carlos in the province of Cáceres in the Extremadura region.

waypoints.name: Confluence of the Tagus and Tiétar rivers
